title: Firms’ Inflation Expectations During the Pandemic-Era Surge - San Francisco Fed
gist: Using the Cleveland Fed's SoFIE panel from 2018-2025, the authors show U.S. firms' inflation expectations broke away from professional forecasters during the pandemic surge and behaved like volatile household expectations, before returning to pre-pandemic characteristics as inflation moderated. Small firms drove the divergence via heightened sensitivity to perceived inflation, and firms' perceptions of the Fed's inflation goal itself drifted up and back down.

themes.theme: Firms decouple from forecasters, track households

insights.claim: Business one-year-ahead inflation expectations closely tracked professional forecasters from 2019 to early 2020, then abruptly disconnected starting early 2021 and spiked to nearly 7% by 2022, mirroring household expectations.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
entities: University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers
insights.so_what: Business expectations are a distinct, more reactive gauge than SPF; when inflation surges, watch firm surveys for signs that price-setters are un-anchoring faster than economists assume.
insights.quote: business expectations abruptly disconnected from professional forecasters’ expectations and increased sharply to nearly 7% by 2022, similar to household expectations during the same period

insights.claim: After CPI inflation peaked in mid-2022, firms' expectations fell faster than households', closely following realized inflation and reverting toward professional forecasters' levels.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Firm expectations are backward-looking and mean-reverting, so they may confirm disinflation rather than lead it, unlike stickier household surveys.
insights.locator: By mid-2022, after CPI inflation peaked

insights.claim: Through end-2025, household inflation expectations stayed elevated and rose further amid tariff-related uncertainty, while firms' expectations continued to decline.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
entities: University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers
insights.so_what: The 2025 divergence flags tariffs as a household-sentiment shock; firms are not (yet) pricing the same inflation risk, a gap worth monitoring for pass-through.
insights.quote: household expectations remained elevated and even rose further amid tariff-related uncertainty, while firms’ expectations continued to decline

insights.claim: Firms' inflation expectations can deviate substantially from professionals' and converge toward households' views, especially when inflation rises sharply.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Policymakers relying mainly on SPF and household surveys may miss the price-setting channel; firm expectations belong in the anchoring dashboard.
insights.locator: The behavior of firms’ inflation expectations

themes.theme: Small firms drive divergence via perception sensitivity

insights.claim: During the surge, inflation expectations of small firms (1-19 employees) rose faster and stronger than those of larger firms (20+ employees), with a gap of roughly 2 percentage points at the 2022 peak.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Firm-size heterogeneity means small-business surveys can overstate aggregate un-anchoring; size-weighting matters for reading the signal.
insights.quote: The difference in inflation expectations between the two groups reached roughly 2 percentage points at the peak of the inflation surge in 2022
insights.locator: Figure 2 shows inflation expectations by firm size

insights.claim: In 2021, a 1 percentage point rise in perceived inflation was associated with a 1 percentage point rise in small firms' one-year-ahead expectations but only 0.2 percentage point for larger firms.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: A near one-for-one pass-through of perceived inflation into small-firm expectations quantifies how quickly price-setters extrapolate, a key input for gauging inflation persistence.
insights.quote: a 1 percentage point increase in perceived inflation was associated with 1 percentage point higher one-year-ahead inflation expectations for small firms but with only 0.2 percentage point higher one-year-ahead expectations for larger firms

insights.claim: The surge in small firms' expectations reflected a temporary spike in their sensitivity to current inflation, and by end-2025 both sensitivity levels and the small-versus-large gap had returned to pre-pandemic levels.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: The un-anchoring was a state-dependent response to a large shock, not a structural break, supporting a benign read on expectations once inflation normalizes.
insights.locator: Our findings suggest that small firms

insights.claim: Firms with incomplete information about economy-wide inflation form expectations shaped by firm-specific perceptions, which can diverge from realized inflation based on location or a firm's mix of production inputs.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: The imperfect-information lens explains why relative-price shocks (energy, inputs) can spill into broad inflation expectations for exposed firms.
insights.locator: Inflation expectations can be shaped by firm-specific perceptions

insights.claim: Firms' longer-term (one-to-five-year average) inflation expectations rose from slightly above 2% in late 2019 to 4.8% in late 2022, then fell to 3.3% by late 2025.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Even long-horizon firm expectations un-anchored materially and remain above 2% into 2025, a residual risk that the last leg of re-anchoring is incomplete.
insights.quote: average longer-term inflation expectations rose from slightly above 2% in late 2019 to 4.8% in late 2022

insights.claim: The deviation in firms' longer-term expectations appeared temporary, receding toward pre-pandemic levels following a cycle of policy rate hikes and the ensuing disinflation.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Aggressive tightening plausibly re-anchored business expectations, evidence that credibility is defensible but requires active policy response, not automatic.
insights.locator: Firms’ longer-term inflation expectations thus deviated

insights.claim: Firms' perceived Federal Reserve inflation goal was stable pre-2021, then rose alongside longer-term expectations in 2022-2023, and fell after 2023 as inflation moderated.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Persistently high inflation can distort firms' beliefs about the 2% target itself, meaning the Fed's perceived goal is not fixed and can erode credibility if inflation lingers.
insights.quote: both longer-term expectations and the perceived inflation goal increased in 2022 and 2023

insights.claim: Higher perceptions of the Fed's inflation goal contributed to higher longer-term inflation expectations, and moderating inflation can restore the accuracy of those goal perceptions.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: Part of the anchoring channel runs through beliefs about the target, so central-bank communication that reaffirms 2% matters for durably lowering firm expectations.

insights.claim: Business inflation expectations directly shape prices, wages, and investment, yet are less frequently monitored than household and professional-forecaster expectations due to a lack of survey data.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: A data gap in policy monitoring; the growing SoFIE panel offers an edge for anticipating price-setting behavior ahead of consensus.

insights.claim: If firms expect higher inflation they may preemptively raise prices, making inflation more persistent.
insights.speaker: Ina Hajdini
insights.so_what: The self-fulfilling channel is why un-anchored firm expectations are a leading risk indicator for sticky inflation and higher-for-longer rates.

top_entities: University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers
